source.title: 2024 ACC/AHA/AACVPR/APMA/ABC/SCAI/SVM/SVN/SVS/SIR/VESS Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease
source.organization: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

signals: Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease

extraction.summary: The 2024 ACC/AHA guideline for management of lower extremity peripheral artery disease provides comprehensive recommendations for PAD across four clinical subsets: asymptomatic, chronic symptomatic, chronic limb-threatening ischemia, and acute limb ischemia. The guideline emphasizes team-based care, medical therapy for cardiovascular risk reduction, structured exercise therapy, and appropriate revascularization strategies.

population.risk_criteria: Patients with suspected or confirmed lower extremity peripheral artery disease
population.disease_definition: Lower extremity peripheral artery disease affecting 10-12 million individuals in the United States
population.severity_staging: Four clinical subsets: 1) Asymptomatic PAD (may have functional impairment), 2) Chronic symptomatic PAD (including claudication), 3) Chronic limb-threatening ischemia, 4) Acute limb ischemia

key_thresholds.context: Primary diagnostic test for PAD detection
Increased risk with atherosclerosis risk factors
Abnormal, diagnostic of PAD when ABI noncompressible
Severe ischemia, associated with major amputation risk
Screening threshold for depression in PAD patients, associated with increased mortality risk
Chronic kidney disease definition as PAD risk amplifier
Threshold for adding PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe
Lower amputation risk threshold in PAD patients with diabetes
Threshold associated with higher major amputation rates

key_thresholds.parameter: Foot ulcer development risk in diabetes

key_thresholds.context: Minimum diameter for adequate conduit in BEST-CLI trial
7-year patency rate for common femoral endarterectomy
Minor or major complications in common femoral endarterectomy
Time constraint for skeletal muscle viability in ALI
Maximum occlusion duration for effective catheter-directed thrombolysis

recommendations.recommendation: Detection of PAD in most patients is accomplished through history, physical examination, and resting ankle-brachial index
Structured exercise is a core component of care for patients with PAD, including supervised exercise therapy and community-based programs
Revascularization should be used to prevent limb loss in chronic limb-threatening ischemia and can improve QOL in claudication patients not responsive to medical therapy and exercise
recommendations.notes: Can be endovascular, surgical, or hybrid approach

recommendations.recommendation: Care for patients with PAD, especially chronic limb-threatening ischemia, is optimized when delivered by a multispecialty care team

In patients at increased risk of PAD, a comprehensive medical history and review of symptoms to assess for exertional leg symptoms, lower extremity rest pain, and lower extremity wounds or other ischemic skin changes should be performed
recommendations.notes: For patients listed in Table 5 risk criteria

recommendations.recommendation: In patients at increased risk of PAD, a comprehensive vascular examination and inspection of the legs and feet should be performed regularly
recommendations.notes: Includes pulse palpation and inspection as detailed in Table 6

recommendations.recommendation: Resting ABI with or without ankle PVR/Doppler waveforms to establish PAD diagnosis in patients with suggestive history/physical exam
Exercise treadmill ABI testing for suspected chronic symptomatic PAD with normal/borderline resting ABI
Toe pressure/TBI when resting ABI >1.40 (noncompressible)
Screening ABI in patients at increased PAD risk
Duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA, or catheter angiography for revascularization planning in functionally limiting claudication
Assess for and incorporate the presence of PAD-related risk amplifiers when developing patient-focused treatment recommendations
recommendations.notes: Risk amplifiers include diabetes, ongoing smoking, CKD, polyvascular disease, microvascular disease, depression, and older age

recommendations.recommendation: Actively pursue evidence of health disparities in diagnosis, treatment, and outcomes for patients with PAD and use efforts to limit the impact of these disparities on clinical outcomes
recommendations.notes: Particularly important for Black, Hispanic, and American Indian populations who have higher amputation rates

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with symptomatic PAD, single antiplatelet therapy is recommended to reduce the risk of MACE
In patients with symptomatic PAD, low-dose rivaroxaban (2.5mg twice daily) combined with low-dose aspirin is effective to reduce the risk of MACE and MALE
After endovascular or surgical revascularization for PAD, antiplatelet therapy is recommended
In older patients (≥75 years) with PAD, assessment for geriatric syndromes can be useful to identify high-risk patients
recommendations.notes: COR 2a - frailty, sarcopenia, malnutrition, mobility impairment

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with PAD without another indication, full-intensity oral anticoagulation should not be used to reduce the risk of MACE and MALE
High-intensity statin therapy with aim of achieving ≥50% reduction in LDL-C
Add PCSK9 inhibitor therapy if LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximal statin therapy
Add ezetimibe therapy if LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximal statin therapy
Antihypertensive therapy to reduce MACE risk
ACE inhibitors or ARBs for hypertension management
Smoking cessation advice at every visit
recommendations.notes: For all tobacco users with PAD

recommendations.recommendation: Pharmacotherapy and counseling for smoking cessation
GLP-1 agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors for PAD patients with type 2 diabetes
Use GLP-1 agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide) and SGLT-2 inhibitors (canagliflozin, dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) in PAD patients with type 2 diabetes
Cilostazol for claudication symptom improvement and increased walking distance
Annual influenza vaccination for PAD patients
Preventive foot self-care education for patients and family members
recommendations.notes: Essential for ulcer and amputation prevention

recommendations.recommendation: Education on foot self-care for patients with PAD and their family members
Supervised exercise therapy for chronic symptomatic PAD to improve walking performance, functional status, and QOL
Structured community-based exercise program with behavioral change techniques for chronic symptomatic PAD
Comprehensive foot evaluation at least annually for patients with PAD
Referral to foot care specialist for ongoing examinations and surveillance

In patients with functionally limiting claudication who are being considered for revascularization, potential benefits with respect to QOL, walking performance, and overall functional status should be weighed against the risks and durability of intervention and possible need for repeated procedures

In patients with functionally limiting claudication and an inadequate response to GDMT (including structured exercise), revascularization is a reasonable treatment option to improve walking function and QOL
In patients with claudication who have had an adequate clinical response to GDMT (including structured exercise), revascularization is not recommended
recommendations.notes: No benefit when GDMT is successful

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant aortoiliac or femoropopliteal disease with inadequate response to GDMT, endovascular revascularization is effective to improve walking performance and QOL
In patients with asymptomatic PAD, revascularization procedures should not be performed solely to prevent progression of disease

In patients undergoing surgical revascularization for functionally limiting claudication and hemodynamically significant femoropopliteal disease, bypass to the popliteal artery with autogenous vein is recommended in preference to prosthetic graft material
In patients with CLTI, a multispecialty care team should evaluate and provide comprehensive care with goals of complete wound healing, minimizing tissue loss, and preservation of ambulatory status

In patients with CLTI, surgical, endovascular, or hybrid revascularization techniques are recommended, when feasible, to minimize tissue loss, heal wounds, relieve pain, and preserve a functional limb
In patients undergoing surgical revascularization for CLTI, bypass to the popliteal or infrapopliteal arteries should be constructed with autogenous vein if available
Patients with CLTI and diabetic foot ulcers should receive pressure offloading, when possible, to promote tissue growth and wound healing
Patients with PAD and previous diabetic foot ulcers should be referred for customized footwear that accommodates, protects, and fits the shape of their feet
In patients with CLTI, prompt management of foot infection with antibiotics, debridement, and other surgical management is recommended
In patients with CLTI with nonhealing wounds, wound care should be provided to optimize the wound-healing environment after revascularization with the goal of complete wound healing

In patients with CLTI who require amputation, evaluation should be performed by a multispecialty care team to assess for the most distal level of amputation that facilitates healing and provides maximal functional ability
Multispecialty care team evaluation for most distal amputation level that facilitates healing and provides maximal functional ability
recommendations.notes: Class 1 recommendation for CLTI patients requiring amputation

recommendations.recommendation: Primary amputation indicated when life over limb is prevailing consideration and limb is cause of patient instability
recommendations.notes: For ischemia, metabolic derangement, or advanced infection

recommendations.recommendation: Revascularization (endovascular or surgical) indicated for ALI with salvageable limb
recommendations.notes: To prevent amputation in categories I, IIa, IIb

recommendations.recommendation: Emergency evaluation by experienced clinician for ALI patients
recommendations.notes: Assess limb viability and implement appropriate therapy

medications.indication: Antiplatelet therapy for cardiovascular risk reduction
Combined with low-dose aspirin to prevent major adverse cardiovascular and limb events in patients not at increased bleeding risk
Single antiplatelet therapy for symptomatic PAD
MACE reduction in PAD with type 2 diabetes
MACE reduction in PAD with type 2 diabetes
MACE reduction in PAD with type 2 diabetes
MACE reduction in PAD with type 2 diabetes
MACE reduction in PAD with type 2 diabetes
Claudication symptoms and walking distance improvement
No-option CLTI patients for pain relief and wound healing

red_flags: Signs of tissue loss or gangrene
Acute limb ischemia with pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesias, paralysis
Chronic limb-threatening ischemia with ischemic rest pain, nonhealing wounds/ulcers, gangrene
Complete sensory loss and motor function loss in acute limb ischemia (Class III - irreversible)
Ischemic rest pain affecting forefoot worsened with limb elevation
Nonhealing or slow-healing lower extremity wound
Advanced CKD in patients requiring gadolinium-enhanced MRA
Signs of acute limb ischemia requiring urgent intervention
Depression symptoms (GDS ≥6) associated with increased MACE and MALE risk
Polyvascular disease with diabetes creating synergistic risk amplification
Severe malnutrition in older patients (5x higher 30-day mortality)
Frailty with high predictive value for 30-day mortality
Dependent functional status in patients ≥70 years with CLTI
High bleeding risk with anticoagulation therapy
Sarcopenia with 10x higher prevalence in PAD patients
Major adverse limb events requiring urgent intervention
Signs of drug-induced myopathy with statins
Congestive heart failure of any severity (contraindication for cilostazol)
Poor glycemic control with hemoglobin A1c >9.5%
History of previous foot ulcers or amputation
Presence of foot ulcers, calluses, or corns
Peripheral neuropathy with loss of protective sensation
Foot deformities (bunions, hammertoe, claw toe, abnormal arch, Charcot deformity)
Development of rest pain or tissue loss indicating CLTI
Life-threatening sepsis from lower extremity ischemia and infection
Limb-threatening infections until complete wound healing is achieved
Foot infection with abscess, gas, or necrotizing fasciitis requiring immediate surgical debridement
Rapid progression of foot infection, particularly in diabetic patients
Extensive tissue loss with exposure of vital structures precluding limb salvage
Life over limb considerations with threatened limb causing patient instability
Life-threatening sepsis due to foot infection requiring immediate amputation
Severe metabolic derangements attributable to extensive tissue necrosis
Loss of both arterial and venous Doppler signals indicating irreversible damage
extraction.follow_up: Longitudinal follow-up required for all PAD patients with regular assessment of cardiovascular risk factors, functional status, and limb symptoms
extraction.escalation_path: Escalate to multispecialty team including vascular specialists, interventional cardiologists, vascular surgeons, podiatrists, and wound care experts for chronic limb-threatening ischemia or when medical therapy and exercise are insufficient for symptom management
